data.title: Rocket Companies, Inc. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Rocket Companies, Inc., a leading player in the mortgage and financial services industry, has released its Form 10-Q report for the third quarter of 2025. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance and operational highlights, reflecting its strategic initiatives and market position.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Revenue: $1,360.3 million, reflecting a slight increase from the previous year, driven by gains on the sale of loans and loan servicing income.
  • Net Income (Loss): $34.1 million, a decrease compared to the previous year, primarily due to increased expenses in salaries, commissions, and team member benefits.
  • Net (Loss) Income Attributable to Rocket Companies: $(1.8) million, indicating a loss attributable to the company after accounting for non-controlling interests.
  • (Loss) Earnings Per Share of Participating Common Stock - Basic: $(0.01), reflecting the net loss attributable to Rocket Companies.
  • (Loss) Earnings Per Share of Participating Common Stock - Diluted: $(0.01), consistent with the basic earnings per share, indicating no dilution effect.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: Rocket Companies operates through two main segments: Direct to Consumer and Partner Network. The Direct to Consumer segment includes revenue from mortgage origination and servicing, while the Partner Network segment focuses on partnerships with real estate agents and other financial institutions.
  • New Product Launches: Rocket Companies completed the acquisition of Redfin Corporation on July 1, 2025. This acquisition is expected to enhance Rocket's offerings in the real estate brokerage space, providing a more comprehensive suite of services to clients.
  • Future Outlook: Rocket Companies anticipates completing the acquisition of Mr. Cooper Group Inc. in the fourth quarter of 2025, subject to regulatory approval. This acquisition is expected to significantly expand Rocket's mortgage servicing capabilities.
  • Operational Strategy: The company continues to focus on leveraging its AI-fueled homeownership strategy to deliver industry-best client experiences across its suite of products, including financial wellness, personal loans, home search, mortgage finance, title, and closing services.
  • Geographical Performance: The company operates primarily in the United States, with some operations in Canada through Rocket Mortgage Canada and Lendesk Technologies.

SEC Filing: Rocket Companies, Inc. [ RKT ] - 10-Q - Aug. 08, 2025

data.title: PAMT CORP SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
PAMT CORP, a leading provider of truckload and logistics services, has released its Form 10-Q report for the second quarter of 2025. The report outlines significant financial and operational challenges faced by the company, including a notable decrease in revenues and an increase in operational costs. This summary provides an overview of the key financial and business highlights from the report.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Operating Revenues: $306.5 million, reflecting a decrease from $365.5 million in the same period last year, primarily due to a reduction in miles traveled and a decrease in rate per mile.
  • Operating Loss: $(20.3) million, compared to $(1.4) million in the prior year, driven by increased depreciation and rent expenses.
  • Net Loss: $(17.8) million, compared to $(2.6) million in the prior year, impacted by higher interest expenses and a decrease in operating revenues.
  • Basic Loss Per Share: $(0.83), compared to $(0.12) in the prior year, reflecting the increased net loss.
  • Diluted Loss Per Share: $(0.83), consistent with basic loss per share, indicating no dilution effect due to the net loss.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: Truckload services accounted for 69.4% of total revenues, excluding fuel surcharges, in Q2 2025, compared to 66.5% in Q2 2024. Brokerage and logistics services made up the remaining 30.6% in Q2 2025, down from 33.5% in Q2 2024.
  • Geographical Performance: In Q2 2025, 55.8% of total revenues, including fuel surcharges, were from domestic U.S. shipments, a decrease from 64.4% in Q2 2024. Shipments to or from Mexico increased to 44.0% of total revenues in Q2 2025, up from 35.4% in Q2 2024.
  • Sales Units: The total miles traveled for truckload services decreased by 11.9% in Q2 2025 compared to Q2 2024, primarily due to a 10.7% reduction in the average number of trucks operated.
  • Operational Challenges: The company faced increased costs in salaries, wages, and benefits, which rose to 41.7% of revenues, before fuel surcharges, in Q2 2025 from 38.6% in Q2 2024. This was due to the fixed-cost nature of employing human capital amidst decreasing revenues.
  • Operational Adjustments: Depreciation expenses increased significantly due to a change in accounting estimates related to the salvage values and useful lives of revenue equipment, impacting the operating ratio.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to purchase approximately 188 new trucks and 650 new trailers in the remainder of 2025, with expected net capital expenditures of approximately $25.1 million.
  • Market Conditions: The ongoing freight recession, characterized by an oversupply of available trucks compared to freight, has led to a reduction in truck count and miles traveled.
  • Strategic Focus: The company is working to amend financial covenant terms with its lender to better align with current market conditions and anticipates having a revised covenant metric in place by the end of Q3 2025.

SEC Filing: PAMT CORP [ PTSI ] - 10-Q - Aug. 08, 2025

data.title: Treasury Yields Rise Ahead of July Inflation Data — Market Talk
data.description:
1554 ET - Increased bets on an interest rate cut by the Fed drive a Treasurys selloff that pushes yields higher for the week, ahead of key inflation data due Tuesday. Markets are pricing two or more cuts this year, but a lot depends on upcoming indicators. Economists surveyed by WSJ expect July 12-month CPI to accelerate to 2.8% from June's 2.7%, with the core rising to 3.1% from 2.9%. Analysts say tariffs are boosting some prices, but the impact on inflation could be temporary. The 10-year rises 0.064 percentage point this week, to 4.282% and the two-year adds 0.056 p.p. to 3.757%. Both are up for the day. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1534 ET - Eli Lilly's weight-loss pill slightly missed the company's efficacy expectations, but shares slid more than they should've, say UBS analysts. The pill led to 12.4% weight loss, which suggests it's still a viable drug. Consumers who want an oral GPL-1 drug may be willing to sacrifice some efficacy to avoid needles. The pill version will also cater to those looking to buy in cash, who value convenience over maximum efficacy, the analysts say. But the lower efficacy could lead Eli Lilly to offer the pill at a discount, which would hurt its potential boost to the company's earnings. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1428 ET [Dow Jones]--Doximity's first-quarter earnings beat shows that its added services are drawing physicians to its platform, which is known as the "LinkedIn for doctors," and pharma ad spending could soon follow, says Mizuho analyst Steven Valiquette. The company's newsfeed reached a record number of users while its AI tool utilization multiplied by five. The company is also seeing strong engagement for AI Scribe, a patient note-taking tool it launched last week. Doximity will "attract greater biopharma digital marketing spend as the app is now reaching rapidly higher and broader levels of physician engagement," Valiquette said. The stock was up 12% to $65.30 Friday. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1401 ET - Maplebear, also known as Instacart, can further leverage its position as a top-two player in grocery delivery, say Oppenheimer analysts who remain bullish on the company. The analysts raised the company's price target to $65 from $60, saying it is showing better new customer retention than last year. Instacart, which will likely see slowed orders from UberEats comparable sales in the third quarter, is expected to see gross transaction value similar to the past four quarters, the analysts say. Shares rise 6.7% at $52.70.(natalie.weger@wsj.com; @NatalieWeger)

1345 ET [Dow Jones]--The number of rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. edged up by one this week to 411, ending a string of 14 weeks of declines, oil service firm Baker Hughes reports. Oil rigs were down by 74 from a year ago. Natural-gas rigs slipped by one to 123 after adding 16 in the previous three weeks. Bank of America strategists Francisco Blanch and Clifton White attribute the climb in natural-gas rigs, led by the Haynesville region, partly to optimism about demand for LNG exports. "It only took 2026 gas prices trading $4-$4.5/mmBtu year to date to increase the natural-gas rig count by more than 20% since 1Q25," they said in a note. "We expect to see incremental production from these newly added gas rigs just as Golden Pass [LNG] enters service by the end of the year." (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1345 ET - Local governments in the U.S. have been flooding the market with new municipal bonds. The added supply has been blamed for a decline in the price --and consequent increase in yields-- of munis. Painful as they may be for bond holders, high yields are an appealing entry point for investors interested in the munis' tax exemption benefit. Conning's Cindy Beualieu expects munis to remain attractive, but at more normal levels. Issuance, which reached a record $513.6 billion last year and is 18% higher year-to-date from a year earlier, is likely to settle around $200 billion a year, "which is reasonable" she says. "We aren't calling for significantly lower rates." (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1342 ET - A Fed cut in September could normalize the Treasurys yield curve, Cunning's Cindy Beaulieu says. Yields are getting higher as maturities get longer, starting with the three-year Treasurys, but not in the front-end of the curve, where shorter maturities are paying more. The anomaly will correct itself with a Fed cut, Beaulieu says. A normal, steepening shape "is positive from the perspective of building income," something appealing to institutional investors such as insurance companies and some pension plans, Beaulieu says. Long-term yields --or 10- to 30-year-- are likely to remain pressured upward as markets worry about budget deficits and how they will be funded, she says. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1340 ET - The Fed faces a thorny dilemma next month, with inflation still running above target while labor indicators weaken. Inflation "is going to be an incredible challenge," Conning's Cindy Beaulieu says. She expects tariffs to push inflation higher for a few months, but "that should be transitory." Meanwhile, inflation expectations remain anchored, despite recent increases, she says. Employment, however, could pave the way for a rate cut. Labor data so far, plus August payrolls to be released before the Fed's next meeting, "will probably give them enough cover to cut 25 basis points." Beaulieu thinks the Fed could leave the door open to pause again after that. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1227 ET - U.S. apartment rent growth continued to slow throughout July, with the average holding at $1,717, Apartments.com says. The month-over-month change was a negligible 0.03% lower, marking the sixth straight month of flat or negative growth. Annual rent growth has also slowed, from 1.5% in January to 1.1% in July. The downward trajectory reflects persistent supply pressures and a cooling demand environment. However, the pace of moderation remains measured, and the national average is still above year-ago levels. Market-level performance diverged in July, with more than half of the nation's 50 largest apartment markets by inventory posting month-over-month rent declines. This prolonged shift could weigh on annual growth figures in the months ahead. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1043 ET - Monster Beverage is benefiting from broad-based strength in the energy drink category, fueled by price convergence with carbonated soft drinks, rising demand for functional beverages and cooler space gains, UBS analysts say in a research note. The company has warned of some tariff-driven pressures in the coming quarters--largely stemming from higher aluminum prices--though mitigation efforts and price increases should cushion this impact. Monster says that pricing actions will be "surgical and dependent on packaging and channel," rather than broad-based across the portfolio. The analysts say that Monster's momentum and fundamental trajectory is favorable, though they note that these factors are likely already priced into the stock. Shares climb 7.8%. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1036 ET - Wendy's cut its 2025 guidance for the second time this year as U.S. sales slump, but the stock isn't getting fried as the burger chain reports pockets of strength elsewhere. The company's profit ticked up to $55.1 million from $54.6 million and adjusted earnings of 29 cents a share topped analyst forecasts by 4 cents, according to FactSet. The company's operating profit was boosted by lower advertising spending, reorganization and realignment costs and overheads. Wendy's also highlighted that its international business delivered 8.7% higher systemwide sales during the quarter. Shares rise 1% to $10.05. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1030 ET - Expedia's stock is set to see the most upside among travel competitors if trends in the U.S. accelerate, Oppenheimer analysts say in a note. Expedia has greater exposure to the U.S. compared to peers like Booking and Airbnb, and management noted broad-based improvement in U.S. travel demand in July, though the low-end consumer is still challenged. Expedia's stock "offers most torque to improving US travel environment," the analysts say, bringing their price target to $250, from $210. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1554 ET - Increased bets on an interest rate cut by the Fed drive a Treasurys selloff that pushes yields higher for the week, ahead of key inflation data due Tuesday. Beaulieu thinks the Fed could leave the door open to pause again after that. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1227 ET - U.S. apartment rent growth continued to slow throughout July, with the average holding at $1,717, Apartments.com says. The month-over-month change was a negligible 0.03% lower, marking the sixth straight month of flat or negative growth. Annual rent growth has also slowed, from 1.5% in January to 1.1% in July. This prolonged shift could weigh on annual growth figures in the months ahead. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1050 ET - Underlying resilience in Canada's labor market is masked by what was effectively a summer jobs report, Scotiabank's Derek Holt argues. The country lost almost 41,000 jobs in July, taking back about half of the prior month's gain. Holt says details of the job survey were mixed, but almost all of the loss was among youths and concentrated in full-time employment. "A flood of temps changed the summer job market and wildfires haven't helped seasonal employment," he says. The jobless rate was steady and wages ripped higher, the economist adds. For the Bank of Canada, Holt says nothing hangs on this one set of numbers. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1047 ET - Canada's labor market snapped back to reality in July with 40,800 jobs lost after a surprising 83,100 were added the month before, Oxford Economics' Michael Davenport says. However, with over a month to go before the Bank of Canada decides on interest rates, the economist doesn't think the job numbers will do much to change policymakers' thinking. He expects the central bank will hold its policy rate at 2.75% given still elevated trade-policy uncertainty. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1045 ET - A weaker-than-expected Canadian employment figure for July supports CIBC Capital Markets call for a quarter percentage point interest rate reduction at the Bank of Canada's September meeting, economist Andrew Grantham says. Still, he notes there is a lot of data due between now and then, including another employment report, two inflation releases and quarterly GDP. For now though, Canada's labor market is adding jobs still but not quickly enough to keep up with population growth, he says.(robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1038 ET - It was a weak start to 3Q for Canada's job market, with employment falling 40,800 in July to largely reverse the shocking 83,100 gain the prior month, BMO Economics' Doug Porter says. He says given the inherent volatility in the jobs figures, it's appropriate to look at a two-month average which would show a 21,000 a month job gain and jobless rate of 6.9%. With July acting as a counterweight to June, the economist says the Bank of Canada would still need to see inflation slow notably over the next two reports for a September interest rate cut to be a high likelihood. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1029 ET - Canada's blockbuster job gains in June look to have been a fluke, as net employment shrinks about 41,000 in July, says Manulife Investment Management's Dominique Lapointe. He notes major job declines were observed across many industries, notably among those not directly impacted by tariffs. As well, job losses were concentrated among youth, a signs of distress in labor demand but not a sign that a broad based recession has started, Lapointe adds. Markets are now pricing in a 40% chance of a Bank of Canada rate cut in September, up from around 17% last week. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1027 ET - The 40,800 slump in Canadian employment in July has Capital Economics' Alexandra Brown a bit more confident the Bank of Canada will resume cutting interest rates next month, though a surprisingly strong CPI report later this month could prompt another pause. Brown says the decline in employment was worryingly concentrated in the private sector, where it was broad-based by industry, and was entirely driven by full-time employment. The jobless rate held steady at 6.9% as falls in employment were more than offset by an unusually large 0.2 percentage point drop in labor force participation, with the labor force declining by 33,000,the economist adds.(robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0957 ET - It's getting harder to predict what action central banks will take, economist James Smith at Dutch bank ING writes. Difference of opinion among rate setters is growing, Smith notes. The Bank of England's policy committee was this week forced into an unprecedented second ballot after failing to reach a majority in the first, following hot on the heels of rare dissent among policymakers at the Fed's last meeting. That's down to the very different conclusions drawn from economic data such as figures on the jobs market, as well as questions over the reliability of such data, Smith says. Rising complexity in supply, including in labor, adds to the volatile mix, while policymakers also differ over the risks of a sustained spike in inflation, he says. "My conclusion... is that we're in for a more volatile period of central-bank expectations," Smith says. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

data.title: 3 Reasons to Sell PEGA and 1 Stock to Buy Instead
data.description:
Pegasystems has been treading water for the past six months, recording a small loss of 2.7% while holding steady at $50.98. The stock also fell short of the S&P 500’s 5.3% gain during that period.

Is now the time to buy Pegasystems, or should you be careful about including it in your portfolio? Get the full breakdown from our expert analysts, it’s free.

Why Is Pegasystems Not Exciting?

We're cautious about Pegasystems. Here are three reasons why there are better opportunities than PEGA and a stock we'd rather own.

1. Long-Term Revenue Growth Disappoints

A company’s long-term sales performance is one signal of its overall quality. Any business can have short-term success, but a top-tier one grows for years. Over the last three years, Pegasystems grew its sales at a 11.1% annual rate. Although this growth is acceptable on an absolute basis, it fell short of our standards for the software sector, which enjoys a number of secular tailwinds.

2. Projected Revenue Growth Is Slim

Forecasted revenues by Wall Street analysts signal a company’s potential. Predictions may not always be accurate, but accelerating growth typically boosts valuation multiples and stock prices while slowing growth does the opposite.

Over the next 12 months, sell-side analysts expect Pegasystems’s revenue to rise by 3.6%, a deceleration versus This projection is underwhelming and implies its products and services will face some demand challenges.

3. Long Payback Periods Delay Returns

The customer acquisition cost (CAC) payback period measures the months a company needs to recoup the money spent on acquiring a new customer. This metric helps assess how quickly a business can break even on its sales and marketing investments.

Pegasystems’s recent customer acquisition efforts haven’t yielded returns as its CAC payback period was negative this quarter, meaning its incremental sales and marketing investments outpaced its revenue. The company’s inefficiency indicates it operates in a competitive market and must continue investing to grow.

Final Judgment

Pegasystems isn’t a terrible business, but it isn’t one of our picks. With its shares trailing the market in recent months, the stock trades at 5.6× forward price-to-sales (or $50.98 per share). Beauty is in the eye of the beholder, but we don’t really see a big opportunity at the moment. We're pretty confident there are superior stocks to buy right now. We’d suggest looking at a dominant Aerospace business that has perfected its M&A strategy.

High-Quality Stocks for All Market Conditions

When Trump unveiled his aggressive tariff plan in April 2025, markets tanked as investors feared a full-blown trade war. But those who panicked and sold missed the subsequent rebound that’s already erased most losses.

Don’t let fear keep you from great opportunities and take a look at Top 5 Growth Stocks for this month. This is a curated list of our High Quality stocks that have generated a market-beating return of 183% over the last five years (as of March 31st 2025).

Stocks that made our list in 2020 include now familiar names such as Nvidia (+1,545% between March 2020 and March 2025) as well as under-the-radar businesses like the once-small-cap company Exlservice (+354% five-year return).

data.title: US yields climb as investors look toward data
data.description:
Key points:
  • Stephen Miran nominated to Federal Reserve board
  • JPMorgan pulls forward Fed cut expectation
  • September Fed cut expectations near 90%

U.S. Treasury yields rose on Friday, with that of the benchmark 10-year note set for its first weekly gain in three weeks after a series of lackluster auctions ahead of next week's inflation data.

Yields have been choppy throughout the week, moving lower on economic data that indicated little movement in the labor market, while a services sector report hinted at a rekindling of inflationary pressures. Yields turned higher later in the session as the Treasury saw weak demand for a total of $125 billion in 3-year notes, 10-year notes and 30-year bonds.

The 10-year yield recorded its biggest weekly drop in two months last week, after a soft government payrolls report sharply increased expectations on the timing and amount of rate cuts from the Federal Reserve this year.

Data next week will include multiple readings on inflation, including the consumer price index (CPI), which will heavily influence rate expectations for the central bank.

The benchmark U.S. 10-year Treasury note yield (US10YT=TWEB) rose 3.9 basis points to 4.283% and was up 6.5 basis points on the week, on track for its biggest weekly gain since early July.

"We probably came down as far as we're likely to, unless we get really much weaker data, and so our call is we stall at 4.25%, said Jay Hatfield, CEO of Infrastructure Capital Management in New York.

"It's normal for this to back up, because we don't know what CPI is going to be," said Hatfield, who also cited the uncertainty surrounding the makeup of the Federal Reserve board of governors.

The yield on the 30-year bond (US30YT=TWEB) rose 4.1 basis points to 4.853% and was up 4.9 basis points on the week after two straight weekly declines.

RATE EXPECTATIONS

Expectations for a rate cut of at least 25 basis points by the Fed at its September meeting stand at 89.4%, according to CME's FedWatch Tool, up from 80.3% a week ago. According to LSEG data, the market is pricing in 58.3 basis points of cuts by the end of the year.

St. Louis Fed President Alberto Musalem said on Friday the Fed's inflation and jobs goals both face risks, with policymakers needing to balance which seems the more serious threat in deciding whether it is appropriate to reduce rates.

A closely watched part of the U.S. Treasury yield curve measuring the gap between two- and 10-year Treasury notes (US2US10=TWEB), seen as an indicator of economic expectations, was at a positive 52.5 basis points.

U.S. President Donald Trump on Thursday said he will nominate Council of Economic Advisers Chairman Stephen Miran to serve out the final few months of a newly vacant seat at the Fed while the White House seeks a permanent addition to the central bank's governing board and continues its search for a new Fed chair.

Miran is replacing Fed Governor Adriana Kugler, who announced a surprise resignation last week, effective on Friday.

In a note to clients, JPMorgan chief U.S. economist Michael Feroli said he now expects the Fed to cut interest rates by 25 basis points at its September meeting, citing signs of weakness in the labor market and uncertainty around Miran's nomination.

The two-year (US2YT=TWEB) U.S. Treasury yield, which typically moves in step with interest rate expectations, advanced 2.2 basis points to 3.756% and was up 5.8 basis points on the week, on pace for its biggest weekly gain since the week ending July 3.

data.title: Former Fugitive Advisor and CNBC Pundit Sentenced to 5 Years for Fraud — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Kenneth Corbin

A former investment advisor and TV personality who made a big bet on a market collapse in 2020 has been sentenced to five years in federal prison for fraud. James Arthur McDonald Jr., 53, pleaded guilty to one count of securities fraud earlier this year stemming from a Ponzi scheme he operated after his investment strategy lost his clients millions of dollars.

Prosecutors say that by effectively betting against the U.S. economy, clients of McDonald's Los Angeles advisory firm Hercules Investments lost between $30 million and $40 million, though there are no criminal allegations tied to that trading strategy. Instead, McDonald's legal troubles sprang from a series of fraudulent activity that began after many of those losses had occurred.

McDonald's lawyers didn't immediately respond to a request for comment on the sentencing.

Throughout 2020 and into 2021, McDonald, who appeared frequently on CNBC, predicted that the presidential election and fallout from the pandemic would cause markets to crater, projecting that the Dow Jones Industrial Average, then above 30,000, would plummet to 15,000.

The Dow took a major hit in March 2020 at the onset of the pandemic, but quickly bounced back and never again fell anywhere near the low that McDonald was predicting. By the end of 2020 Hercules clients were complaining about their losses.

In early 2021, McDonald began soliciting investments for what he described as a capital raise for his advisory firm, but failed to disclose the massive losses his clients had sustained. He also misrepresented how he would use the money, some of which he put toward personal expenses, including nearly $110,000 in rent payments and nearly $175,000 spent at a Porsche dealership, according to prosecutors.

McDonald was also accused of defrauding clients at another firm he controlled, Index Strategy Advisors, raising $3.6 million purportedly for trading purposes. Instead, he spent that money on various personal expenses and Ponzi-like repayments to earlier investors.

McDonald's story took a dramatic turn after he failed to appear before the Securities and Exchange Commission in November 2021 to testify about the alleged fraud and was deemed a fugitive. McDonald was arrested in June 2024 at a residence in Port Orchard, Wash. There, law enforcement found a phony Washington, D.C., driver's license with McDonald's picture bearing the name Brian Thomas. He has been in custody since his arrest.

Prosecutors say McDonald's fraud cost his customers more than $3 million in losses. In April 2024, a federal court ordered McDonald to pay $3.8 million in disgorgement and prejudgment interest in a civil case brought by the SEC, and ordered him and his firm to pay roughly $5 million in civil penalties.

U.S. District Judge Dale S. Fischer of California's central district, who presided over the criminal case, is planning to order a restitution hearing at a later date.

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Trump fires IRS commissioner, Bessent named acting head
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump is removing Billy Long as the commissioner of the Internal Revenue Service and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent will temporarily serve as acting commissioner, a White House official and a source familiar with the matter said on Friday.

The New York Times, which first reported the news, said Long, a former congressman from Missouri, was expected to be nominated for an ambassador post, but did not name the country.

"Secretary Bessent will now serve as acting commissioner of the IRS," the White House official said.

A Treasury spokesperson declined to comment.

Long was sworn in as the 51st Commissioner of the IRS in June, with his term meant to run through November 2027.

data.title: LEE ENTERPRISES, Inc SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
LEE ENTERPRISES, Inc, a leading provider of local news and information in mid-sized communities across the United States, has released its Form 10-Q report for the third quarter of 2025. The report highlights the company's financial performance and operational achievements amidst ongoing business transformation efforts, particularly focusing on digital growth and cost management.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Operating Revenue: $141.3 million, decreased by 6.2% compared to the same quarter last year due to declines in print advertising and impacts from a cybersecurity incident.
  • Operating Income: $4.7 million, a slight decrease from $4.9 million in the prior year quarter, reflecting cost management efforts despite revenue challenges.
  • Net Loss: $1.7 million, improved from a net loss of $3.7 million in the prior year quarter, driven by cost reductions and efficiency improvements.
  • Loss Per Common Share: $(0.31), improved from $(0.73) in the prior year quarter, reflecting the company's efforts in cost management and operational efficiency.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: Digital advertising and marketing services accounted for 73.8% of total advertising and marketing services revenue in the 2025 Quarter, showing a slight increase from 72.5% in the same period last year.
  • Revenue Segments: Digital-only subscription revenue grew by 13.4% in the 2025 Quarter compared to the 2024 Quarter, with digital-only subscribers now totaling 670,000.
  • Revenue Segments: Digital services revenue increased by 3.5% in the 2025 Quarter compared to the 2024 Quarter, highlighting growth in this segment.
  • Geographical Performance: The company operates in 72 mid-sized local communities across 25 states, focusing on providing local news and information.
  • Sales Units: The company has a rapidly growing digital subscription platform with 670,000 digital subscribers, indicating a strong shift towards digital consumption.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to continue its business transformation efforts, focusing on digital growth and reducing operating expenses, particularly in its print businesses.
  • Future Outlook: The company anticipates that funds necessary for capital expenditures, expected to total up to $7.0 million in 2025, will be available from internally generated funds.

SEC Filing: LEE ENTERPRISES, Inc [ LEE ] - 10-Q - Aug. 08, 2025

data.title: Gen Digital Gains After Increase to Full-Year Revenue Guidance
data.description:
By Natalie Weger

Shares of Gen Digital rose after the business-solutions company boosted its full-year adjusted revenue guidance, and reported higher profit and revenue in the first quarter.

The stock was up 9%, at $30.77, late Friday and is 13% higher for the year.

The company, whose brands include Norton, Avast and Lifelock, on Thursday increased its full-year 2026 adjusted revenue guidance to between $4.8 billion and $4.9 billion, compared with its previous outlook of between $4.7 billion and $4.8 billion.

Gen Digital posted a first-quarter profit of $135 million, or 22 cents a share, compared with $181 million, or 29 cents a share, last year.

Adjusted per-share earnings were 64 cents, beating the 60 cents expected by analysts polled by FactSet.

Revenue increased by 30%, to $1.26 billion, beating analyst expectations of $1.19 billion.

The Tempe, Ariz., company expects full-year adjusted earnings per share between $2.49 and $2.56, up from its prior guidance of $2.46 and $2.54. Wall Street expects $2.52.

Write to Natalie Weger at natalie.weger@wsj.com

data.title: Apple shares head for biggest week since 2020 following investment pledge
data.description:
** Shares of Apple Inc jumping ~4% on Fri, with the shares on track for strongest weekly pct gain in five years after the iPhone maker on Wed pledged $100 bln in additional US investment

** AAPL up ~13% for the week after U.S. President Donald Trump on Wed announced the investment, which centers on expanding the co's supply chain footprint in the U.S. but still falls short of Trump's demand that AAPL make iPhones domestically

** Appearing at the White House, AAPL CEO Tim Cook also gave Trump a U.S.-made souvenir with a 24-karat gold base

** For the week, AAPL's market cap has gained ~$390 bln, lifting its market cap to $3.39 trln

data.title: Trump health commission expected to miss its deadline, Bloomberg News reports
data.description:
A federal commission established under the Trump administration is expected to miss next week’s deadline for releasing a highly anticipated report on improving children’s health, Bloomberg News reported on Friday, citing people familiar with the discussions.

The Make America Healthy Again (MAHA) report—originally scheduled for release by August 12—seeks to highlight what the Department of Health has described as a “chronic disease epidemic” affecting both children and adults across the United States.

The commission was established by an executive order signed by President Donald Trump on February 13, 2025 and is chaired by the Secretary of Health and Human Services, Robert F. Kennedy Jr.

Its mandate includes developing broad policy recommendations intended to guide multiple federal agencies, including the Food and Drug Administration (FDA), National Institutes of Health (NIH), and Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC).

data.title: ICE Review: Canola Drops to End Week
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--The ICE Futures canola market was weaker on Friday, taking back Thursday's gains as losses in Chicago soyoil and improved moisture conditions in parts of Western Canada weighed on values.

Chart-based speculative selling was a feature, with the November contract settling below its 100-day moving average.

European rapeseed was also lower, while Malaysian palm oil and the Canadian dollar held relatively steady.

Tight old crop supplies and solid end user demand remained supportive underneath the market.

There were 32,074 contracts traded on Friday, which compares with Thursday, when 47,389 contracts changed hands.

Spreading accounted for 16,974 of the contracts traded.

data.title: DC facing $20 million security funding cut despite Trump complaints of US capital crime
data.description:
The Trump administration plans to slash security funding for Washington, D.C., according to a grant notice, even as President Donald Trump asserts crime in the U.S. capital is “totally out of control.”

The Federal Emergency Management Agency said in a notice posted last week that D.C. and surrounding areas would receive $20 million less this year from its urban security fund, reducing the funding to $25.2 million and amounting to a 44% year-on-year cut.

The Department of Homeland Security, which oversees FEMA, said on Friday it cut funds to D.C. and other cities to align with the “current threat landscape.”

The agency has "observed a shift from large-scale, coordinated attacks like 9/11 to simpler, small-scale assaults, heightening the vulnerability of soft targets and crowded spaces in urban areas,” it said.

FEMA also cut security money for Chicago, New York City, Los Angeles, Jersey City and San Francisco, but the reduction in D.C. was the largest for any urban area that received funding from the program last fiscal year.

D.C. Mayor Muriel Bowser’s office did not immediately respond to a Reuters email requesting comment on the funding cuts.

The federal security funding covers needs in the National Capital Region, an area that includes D.C. and nearby cities in Maryland and Virginia.

Regional officials in the past have used the money to pay for hazmat training, hiring officers and replacing fiber in their emergency communications network, according to a 2016 report from D.C.'s Homeland Security and Emergency Management Agency.

FEMA has $553.5 million to spend to help cities across the U.S. shore up their security. Reuters could not immediately verify how much of the National Capital Region's total security budget comes from that program.

Trump directed federal police on Thursday to step up their presence in the capital, days after staffer Edward Coristine was attacked in D.C. It was the president's latest step toward taking over running the city that is the seat of the U.S. government.

Violent crime in D.C. dropped 35% between 2023 and 2024, according to a December report from the U.S. Attorney’s Office for D.C.

data.title: Cohen & Co Acquires Gioffre & Company, LLP
data.description:
CLEVELAND, Aug. 8, 2025 /PRNewswire/ — Gioffre & Company, LLP (Gioffre) has joined Cohen & Co, one of the top tax and accounting firms in the U.S. Based in Westchester County, New York, Gioffre is a full-service accounting firm with over 50 years of combined experience. The transaction closed on August 8, 2025.

Gioffre was founded by Anthony Gioffre, who also served as its managing partner, and includes five additional employees. The firm brings outsourced accounting, financial reporting and tax expertise from working with both public and large private company clients nationwide.

"Through our many discussions with Anthony, we have gained tremendous confidence in the strategic, positive impact this will have on our firm, " says Partner in Charge, Markets & Industries Marcy Kempf. "Adding such a talented team will help us continue to enhance and expand our CFO support, financial reporting and tax provision services, delivering tailored solutions to meet the needs of our clients and their investors."

"We are excited for this new venture with Cohen & Co and chose this move with intention," says Anthony Gioffre. "Our goal is to provide enhanced service opportunities and bench strength to our long-standing clients and to offer longevity for our employees at a highly respected platform firm."

With the addition of Gioffre, Cohen & Co reaffirms its commitment to deliver high-quality assurance, tax and advisory solutions that reflect the diverse needs of the New York Tri-State region. The Westchester office joins a growing network of locations strategically positioned to support clients locally, regionally and nationally.

Calfee, Halter & Griswold LLP served as legal counsel to Cohen & Co for this transaction. This is the third acquisition for Cohen & Co in 2025, following Cleveland-based Tax & Wealth Management, Inc. in January and Chicago-based Tassi and Company in March.

About Cohen & Co

Named one of America's Most Recommended Tax and Accounting Firms by USA TODAY and one of the Best of the Best Firms by INSIDE Public Accounting, Cohen & Co offers assurance, tax and advisory services to clients throughout the U.S. and worldwide. The firm serves a broad range of clients, from privately held companies and their owners; to public and private funds, advisers and fund service providers within the investment industry; to Fortune 1000 multinational enterprises. Founded in 1977, Cohen & Co has more than 800 dedicated professionals across the U.S. and 15 offices in Colorado, Illinois, Ohio, Maryland, Michigan, New York, Pennsylvania and Wisconsin. Through an affiliated entity, the firm also has a presence in the Cayman Islands. Learn more at cohenco.com.

"Cohen & Co" is the brand name under which Cohen & Company, Ltd. and Cohen & Co Advisory, LLC, and its subsidiary entities, provide professional services. Cohen & Company, Ltd. and Cohen & Co Advisory, LLC practice in an alternative practice structure in accordance with the AICPA Code of Professional Conduct and applicable law, regulations and professional standards. Cohen & Company, Ltd. is a licensed independent CPA firm that provides attest services to its clients. Cohen & Co Advisory, LLC and its subsidiary entities provide tax, advisory and business consulting services to their clients and are not licensed CPA firms. The entities operating under the Cohen & Co brand are independently owned and are not responsible for the services provided by any other entity operating under the Cohen & Co brand. Our use of terms such as "our firm," "we," "us" and other terms of similar import denote the alternative practice structure of Cohen & Company, Ltd. and Cohen & Co Advisory, LLC.
